title: C# Asynchronous Programming Workshop
headline: Spoiler: Just Adding the async Keyword to a Method Doesn't Make It Asynchronous
subheadline: A live, hands-on workshop where you'll finally understand what async actually does under the hood - and write asynchronous C# code that's correct, predictable and deadlock-free.
painPoints: You added the async keyword to your method and assumed it's now asynchronous - but nothing actually changed
Your app freezes or deadlocks and you can't figure out why - .Result and .Wait() seem fine until they aren't
You don't really know when to use await vs ContinueWith - or what happens when you have to handle exceptions in async code
async void, fire-and-forget, swallowed exceptions - your code runs but you're never fully confident it's correct
transformation: Understand exactly what async/await does behind the scenes - no more cargo-cult coding
Know when to use async for I/O-bound work vs parallel for CPU-bound work - and never confuse them again
Handle exceptions, cancellation tokens and continuations like a developer who's actually read the docs
Write async code that doesn't deadlock, doesn't swallow errors and doesn't surprise you in production
whatYouWillDo: Break down what the await keyword actually does - and understand what really makes a method asynchronous
Build your own asynchronous methods from scratch and understand the state machine and performance implications
Use the Task Parallel Library - chain work with ContinueWith, apply TaskContinuationOptions for success, failure and cancellation paths
Run multiple tasks in parallel with Task.WhenAll and collect all results cleanly
Handle exceptions properly - compare exception behaviour with await vs ContinueWith and stop losing stack traces
Implement CancellationTokens to gracefully cancel long-running operations
Diagnose and fix real deadlock scenarios caused by .Result and .Wait() - understand why they happen and how to avoid them
Understand the SynchronizationContext and why ConfigureAwait(false) matters
Walk through a best practices checklist and performance considerations you can apply to your codebase immediately
Complete practical exercises with live corrections so you leave knowing - not just believing your code is right
prerequisites: You should be comfortable with Object Oriented Programming in C#. You don't need any prior experience with async/await or the Task Parallel Library - that's exactly what we'll build together.
whyDifferent: Every concept is coded live with real examples - not explained on slides you'll forget by next week
Hands-on exercises after each topic, with live corrections so you understand exactly where and why your code broke
The sandbox environment stays available after the workshop - keep experimenting with the same examples and exercises
Recordings stay accessible for a period so you can revisit any explanation at your own pace
Cheatsheets provided - a practical async/await reference you'll keep reaching for long after the workshop
Maximum 15 participants so you get real attention, real answers and real feedback - not a seat in a crowd
Delivered in a mix of Mauritian Creole and English - designed so the concepts actually stick
A badge to showcase your new skills on LinkedIn and get noticed by employers

instructor.name: Mehfooz Kausmally
instructor.description: A seasoned software professional from Mauritius with over a decade of experience in the IT industry. After a successful career as a developer in multinational companies working extensively with C#, .NET and complex backend systems, Mehfooz transitioned into full-time professional coaching. He has already guided over 270 engineers across different technologies and levels - bringing real-world expertise and a practical, hands-on approach into every session.
